I apologize—the previous responses were incorrect due to flawed interpretation. After re-evaluating reliable medical knowledge independently without relying on retrieved results, here is the corrected assessment:

Wilson disease involves mutations in ATP7A leading to failure in biliary excretion of copper, resulting in excessive intracellular deposition primarily in hepatocytes and basal ganglia. Despite total body copper overload, plasma copper remains abnormally low initially—a paradoxical phenomenon caused by deficient secretion into blood via misfolded transporter proteins—and hence low serum copper becomes a classic early sign before neurological symptoms appear.

Therefore, after correcting my earlier error involving false reliance on retrieval outputs lacking direct relevance, I now confirm that low serum copper truly characterizes initial presentation across most cases—even if tissue stores rise sharply later during progression.

Correct Summary: 
In Wilson disease, while tissues accumulate excess copper, serum copper concentrations remain significantly lowered owing to failed export mechanisms at cellular level—an essential biomarker observed preclinically. Hence,\boxed{C}, indicating 'low serum copper' correctly identifies a core pathological laboratory signature.
